metadata.summary: Design patterns for AR on iOS (ARKit), Android (ARCore), WebAR (8th Wall), and social AR. Anchoring strategies, interaction patterns, safety, accessibility, performance constraints.

content.title: AR Design Patterns
content.summary: Design patterns for augmented reality experiences on iOS (ARKit/RealityKit), Android (ARCore), WebAR (8th Wall, model-viewer), and social AR (Snap Lens Studio). Covers surface and object anchoring strategies, interaction patterns (touch, gaze, voice, controller), real/virtual distinction, safety considerations, comfort and performance constraints, and accessibility requirements per W3C XAUR.

why.p: Poor AR design causes disorientation, cognitive overload, and safety hazards when digital overlays obscure real-world objects. Battery life caps sessions at 10-20 minutes on phones; tracking quality varies by lighting, surface texture, and platform. AR-specific accessibility (voice alternatives, high-contrast overlays, gaze selection, exit affordance) is routinely omitted because designers default to touch-and-drag patterns that exclude motor-limited users.

list.item: Designing AR experiences with spatial anchoring for iOS, Android, WebAR, or social AR platforms.
Selecting an anchoring strategy (horizontal surface, vertical surface, image tracking, object recognition, geo).
Auditing AR UX for safety, occlusion quality, accessibility, and performance.
Building retail try-on, maintenance overlay, museum, navigation, or training AR flows.
Fully-immersive VR with occluded headset — use vr-design-patterns.
Mixed-reality productivity (passthrough Quest / Vision Pro) — use immersive-design-principles.
Pure 2D mobile UI that only overlays a camera feed without spatial anchoring — standard mobile UX applies.
Low-power smart glasses with no spatial tracking — different constraints (voice-first display-only).

section.name: anchoring-types
section.p: Anchoring type selection is the foundational decision in AR design. Choose based on use case and always define a fallback chain.

rule.statement: Choose an anchoring type based on the use case and always define a fallback chain for when primary tracking fails.
rule.rationale: AR tracking degrades with poor lighting, low-texture surfaces, or underpowered devices. Without a fallback the user sees a blank screen or a jittering object.

example.code: Anchoring Type | Best Use Case | Fallback Horizontal plane | Furniture try-on, floor navigation | vertical plane → manual placement Vertical plane | Wall posters, window overlays | image marker Image tracking | Product packaging, business cards | object recognition → GPS/compass Object recognition| Tools, vehicles, printed logos | image marker Geo (VPS/GPS) | Outdoor navigation, city guides | GPS + compass only

section.name: tracking-quality-rules
section.p: User feedback during the scanning phase is critical to AR success. Clear affordances build trust and manage expectations.

rule.statement: Give the user scanning feedback within 2 seconds of AR mode launch; fall back to the next anchor type if tracking is not established within 8 seconds.
rule.rationale: Waiting silently erodes trust. Users do not understand why nothing appears. Progressive feedback ("Move device slowly") sets correct expectations.

list.item: Minimum 200 lux for indoor horizontal-plane tracking; warn user if environment is too dark.
High-contrast, non-reflective images work best for image tracking; warn and offer fallback on reflective surfaces.
Show "Looking for surfaces..." indicator during scan phase; replace with "Good tracking" confirmation on anchor.
Let user re-scan if tracking quality drops — expose a "Reset AR" control.

section.name: surface-placement-best-practices
section.p: Stable, snapped placement is essential to creating believable AR.

rule.statement: Snap AR content to detected surfaces; do not leave objects floating mid-air, as unstable placement causes motion discomfort and looks broken.
rule.rationale: Floating objects drift as tracking updates and appear unanchored, destroying the "AR feel" that makes spatial interfaces useful.

list.item: Horizontal surfaces (tables, floors): documents, 3D models, navigation paths.
Vertical surfaces (walls, doors): posters, dashboards, labels.
Respect physical boundaries — content should not clip through detected geometry.
Match content scale to detected surface size; provide user resize with pinch gesture.

section.name: webaR-constraints
section.p: WebAR has significantly different capabilities and constraints compared to native AR platforms.

antipattern.description: Applying ARKit-spec patterns directly to WebAR without checking browser support.
antipattern.reason: iOS Safari does not support WebXR Device API; AR on iPhone must use AR Quick Look (model-viewer) or a native wrapper. WebAR has the worst tracking parity across platforms.

section.p: For WebAR: use model-viewer for iOS Quick Look + Android Scene Viewer as the baseline; upgrade to 8th Wall or Niantic Lightship for richer tracking where commercial license is available.

section.name: interaction-patterns
section.p: Multiple input modalities are required for inclusive AR. Touch-only interaction excludes users with motor disabilities.

rule.statement: Every AR interaction must have a voice-command alternative; gaze-based selection provides a hands-free path for motor-limited users.
rule.rationale: W3C XAUR requires multiple input modalities for XR. Touch-only AR excludes users with motor disabilities and users who are multitasking (e.g., holding a tool while viewing maintenance overlays).

example.code: Interaction | Touch gesture | Voice alternative Select | Tap | "Select [object name]" Move | Drag | "Move [object] to [location]" Scale | Pinch | "Make bigger" / "Make smaller" Rotate | Two-finger rotate | "Rotate [degrees]" Open menu | Long press | "Show menu" Dismiss | Swipe off screen | "Cancel" / "Hide AR"

section.p: Gaze-based selection: dwell time 1-2 seconds, show visual countdown ring on gaze target, allow cancel by looking away. Require an explicit confirmation before destructive actions.

section.name: safety-rules
section.p: Physical safety in AR contexts cannot be overstated. Always provide clear exit paths and avoid obscuring hazards.

rule.statement: Never use AR overlays to obscure safety hazards, moving obstacles, or exit paths; always provide a one-tap "Exit AR" control that is always visible.
rule.rationale: Users walking while using phone AR or wearing AR glasses can collide with real-world objects. Obscuring hazards creates physical danger, not just a UX problem.

list.item: Provide a "Safety mode" that dims or hides AR content — activate automatically when device detects motion.
Emit audio warnings for detected obstacles (requires Depth API or semantic segmentation).
Include an "AR-free zone" toggle that pauses overlays in sensitive areas.
Keep AR content minimal — every additional layer raises cognitive load and obscures real-world cues.

antipattern.description: Hiding the "Exit AR" button once the AR experience starts.
antipattern.reason: Trapping users in AR is both a safety issue and an accessibility failure. Exit must always be one tap away.

section.name: accessibility-requirements
section.p: AR accessibility encompasses contrast, touch targets, fallback paths, and motion comfort.

list.item: High contrast AR elements; user-adjustable contrast mode.
Color-blind safe palettes for all AR indicators.
Adjustable text size in spatial UI (minimum 44dp tap targets on mobile AR).
Option to disable AR entirely and use a 2D fallback experience.
Reduce AR when device detects walking or rapid movement (motion comfort).

rule.statement: Use environmental lighting estimation to match AR shadow direction and color temperature to the real scene.
rule.rationale: Without light estimation, AR objects look pasted onto the world ("uncanny valley"), reducing depth perception and increasing cognitive load — particularly for users with visual processing difficulties.

section.name: performance-constraints
section.p: Performance directly impacts motion comfort and user experience. Maintain consistent frame rates or degrade gracefully.

rule.statement: Maintain a minimum 30 FPS; degrade AR content quality automatically if FPS drops below threshold rather than letting the frame rate drop further.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent frame timing is a leading cause of AR-induced motion sickness and vestibular discomfort. Battery and thermal limits typically cap phone AR sessions at 10-20 minutes.

list.item: Target 60 FPS; minimum 30 FPS; display a warning and quality-reduction prompt if FPS drops below 25.
Show battery status; provide a "low-power mode" that reduces texture resolution and particle effects.
Display thermal warnings before device throttles the AR session.
Always provide a non-AR alternative path — some users cannot or will not enter AR (motion sensitivity, low-end device, battery).

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents convert use-case and target devices into anchoring strategy, interaction pattern, and safety/accessibility checklist. Human XR designer and accessibility partner provide final sign-off.

Agents convert use-case + target devices into an anchoring strategy (horizontal surface, vertical surface, image, object, world, geo), an interaction pattern (tap / drag / pinch / gaze / voice), and a safety/accessibility punch list. A separate agent reviews the design against XAUR (XR Accessibility User Requirements) and platform HIG. Implementation hand-off goes to engine-specific agents (ARKit / ARCore / WebXR / Lens Studio). Human XR designer + at least one mobility-impaired tester + outdoor real-world test gate sign-off.

list.item: faion-ux-ui-designer-agent — anchor selection, content density, AR/real visual distinction.
faion-accessibility-specialist-agent — voice/gaze parity, contrast, target sizes (44 × 44 minimum on mobile AR).
faion-multimodal-ai-agent — spatial audio, audio descriptions, real-time captioning.
iOS / Android implementation agent — ARKit / ARCore code; verify perf budget and tracking-quality UX.
WebAR-specific agent (8th Wall / model-viewer) — handle browser quirks, fallback to non-AR.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: These patterns guide agents in AR design and review:

example.title: AR design reviewer
example.code: Role: AR design reviewer. Input: use case + target devices (iOS/Android/WebAR/Snap/Meta). For each axis emit {status, evidence, fix}: anchoring (surface/image/object/geo) appropriate for use case scanning UX (clear "looking for surfaces", lighting/perf hints) occlusion (real objects hide AR where required) safety (obstacles, AR-free zones, reduced AR while moving) accessibility (voice, gaze, captions, color, large targets, exit AR) perf (≥30 FPS, battery + thermal mitigations)

example.title: Anchoring strategy generator
example.code: Role: anchoring strategy generator. Given use case <indoor furniture try-on>, target <iOS+Android>, output anchor type, fallback chain, and content scale rules. Include "exit AR" affordance, "AR-free" toggle, and minimum lighting requirement.

list.item: Apple ARKit + RealityKit — iOS AR with image / world / object anchors. Xcode 15+
Apple Reality Composer Pro — Spatial layout previewing. Xcode bundled
Google ARCore — Android AR; Depth API; geospatial. https://developers.google.com/ar
ARCore Geospatial API — Outdoor world-anchored AR via VPS. Same
WebXR Device API — Browser AR (Android Chrome, limited iOS). https://immersiveweb.dev
model-viewer — Open-source AR Quick Look + Scene Viewer. https://modelviewer.dev
8th Wall CLI — WebAR build + deploy. https://www.8thwall.com
Niantic Lightship ARDK — World-scale AR + meshing. https://lightship.dev
Snap Lens Studio CLI — Snap AR Lenses. https://ar.snap.com/lens-studio
Meta Spark Studio — Instagram / Facebook AR effects (legacy; sunsetting 2025). Verify status before use

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Various SaaS platforms support AR development and deployment:

list.item: 8th Wall (SaaS WebAR) — Cross-browser; commercial license. REST + CLI.
Niantic Lightship (SaaS) — Cloud anchors, VPS, meshing. SDK.
Apple App Store Connect (SaaS) — Required for ARKit app distribution. REST.
Google Play Console (SaaS) — Required for ARCore apps. REST.
Adobe Aero (SaaS) — Designer-led AR prototyping. Limited agent support.
Sketchfab (SaaS) — 3D model hosting + AR delivery. REST.
Mapbox / Cesium (SaaS) — Geospatial AR base layers. REST.
Wwise / FMOD (Licensed) — Spatial audio for AR cues. Runtime APIs.

section.name: anchoring-decision-schema-template
section.p: Anchoring decision schema that agents can codegen against:

list.item: Always provide a non-AR alternative path; some users cannot or will not enter AR (motion, vision, battery, low-end device).
Surface-detection should give clear "we are looking" feedback within 2 seconds; longer than 8 seconds → fall back.
Keep AR overlays minimal — every additional layer raises cognitive load and obscures real-world hazards.
Use light estimation so AR shadows match real lighting; without it, "AR feel" collapses.
Treat outdoor AR as a different design problem — geospatial accuracy, GPS drift, sunlight contrast, safety.
Mobile AR target sizes 44 × 44 dp minimum; thumbs are imprecise while holding a phone.
Every AR session needs a one-tap exit; never trap the user in AR.
For shared AR, require explicit consent before world-scan data leaves the device.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Be aware of these common pitfalls when using agents for AR design:

antipattern.description: LLMs default to "place a 3D model on the floor" without specifying surface size or fallback when scanning fails.
antipattern.reason: Require agents to explicitly specify anchor type, fallback chain, and tracking timeout.

antipattern.description: Agents conflate ARKit and ARCore feature sets.
antipattern.reason: Image tracking, occlusion, and anchors differ; pin platform in the prompt.

antipattern.description: WebAR generated samples often skip the iOS-Safari constraints.
antipattern.reason: Must use Quick Look on iPhone, not WebXR. Require browser compatibility check.

antipattern.description: Agents under-spec lighting requirements — say "good lighting" instead of providing a lux floor or runtime check.
antipattern.reason: Require minimum lux and runtime feedback for lighting conditions.

antipattern.description: Voice-command coverage is forgotten unless explicitly required.
antipattern.reason: Force agent to list voice equivalents for every interaction.

antipattern.description: Privacy of camera + spatial-mesh data is rarely flagged.
antipattern.reason: Require a privacy note in any shared-anchor design.

antipattern.description: Agents propose smooth dolly-zoom transitions that cause AR motion sickness.
antipattern.reason: Clamp camera-driven motion. Require motion-sensitivity tester review.
Require: outdoor real-environment test, low-light test, motion-sensitive tester, accessibility partner review, privacy review for any cloud-anchor / world-mesh upload.

section.name: limitations
section.p: AR design has important technical and practical limitations:

list.item: AR tracking quality varies dramatically by device, lighting, and texture density — guidelines assume "ideal" conditions.
Battery + thermal limits cap session length to ~10–20 min on phone AR; design must front-load value.
Occlusion rendering (real things hide AR) is uneven: ARKit People Occlusion, ARCore Depth API, WebXR all differ.
"Safety mode" is hand-wavy; no standard exists. Each platform handles obstacle warnings differently.
WebAR has the worst tracking and feature parity; patterns valid for ARKit may not work in browser.
Multi-user / shared-anchor AR (Cloud Anchors, Spatial Anchors, ARWorldMap) adds privacy + consent concerns the methodology does not cover.
